mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {K} be a bicategory, and let A \equiv { \mathopen {} \left ( l_A \dashv r_A \colon A_1 \to A_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} and B \equiv { \mathopen {} \left ( l_B \dashv r_B \colon B_1 \to B_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} be a pair of adjunctions in \mathcal {K}. Then a (left and right) lax morphism { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } between the two adjunctions is given by a pair of maps { A_0 } \xrightarrow {{ f_0 }}{ B_0 } and { A_1 } \xrightarrow {{ f_1 }}{ B_1 } together with a pair of mates { l_B \circ f_0 } \xrightarrow {{ l_f }}{ f_1 \circ l_A } and { f_0 \circ r_A } \xrightarrow {{ r_f }}{ r_B \circ f_1 } as depicted below:

ol.li: When the left mate is invertible, we say that { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } is (left strong) and right lax.
When the right mate is invertible, we say that { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } is (right strong) and left lax.
When both mates are invertible, we say that { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } is strong.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {K} be a bicategory, and let A \equiv { \mathopen {} \left ( l_A \dashv r_A \colon A_1 \to A_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} and B \equiv { \mathopen {} \left ( l_B \dashv r_B \colon B_1 \to B_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} be a pair of adjunctions in \mathcal {K}. Fix a pair of morphisms { A_0 } \xrightarrow {{ f_0 }}{ B_0 } and { A_1 } \xrightarrow {{ f_1 }}{ B_1 }.

Let \mathcal {K} be a bicategory, and let A \equiv { \mathopen {} \left ( l_A \dashv r_A \colon A_1 \to A_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} and B \equiv { \mathopen {} \left ( l_B \dashv r_B \colon B_1 \to B_0 \right ) \mathclose {}} be a pair of adjunctions in \mathcal {K}. Then a (left and right) lax morphism { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } between the two adjunctions is given by a pair of maps { A_0 } \xrightarrow {{ f_0 }}{ B_0 } and { A_1 } \xrightarrow {{ f_1 }}{ B_1 } together with a pair of mates { l_B \circ f_0 } \xrightarrow {{ l_f }}{ f_1 \circ l_A } and { f_0 \circ r_A } \xrightarrow {{ r_f }}{ r_B \circ f_1 } as depicted below:

ol.li: When the left mate is invertible, we say that { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } is (left strong) and right lax.
When the right mate is invertible, we say that { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } is (right strong) and left lax.
When both mates are invertible, we say that { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } is strong.

frontmatter.title: Strong morphisms of adjunctions and the Beck–Chevalley condition

mainmatter.p: The condition that a lax morphism of adjunctions be right strong is sometimes referred to as the Beck–Chevalley condition, e.g. by Uemura. Indeed, the right-hand mates in a lax morphism of adjunctions are occasionally referred to as Beck–Chevalley transformations.
A bicategory B is given by the following structure and axioms:

ol.li: a collection of objects x,y,z called 0-cells;
for each pair of 0-cells x,y a category \mathbf {hom} _{ B } { \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {}} of morphisms or 1-cells x \xrightarrow {f}y;
for each object x, a morphism x \xrightarrow { 1 _{ x } }x called the identity morphism;

for all objects x,y,z, a functor \mathbf {hom} _{ B } { \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {}} \times \mathbf {hom} _{ B } { \mathopen {} \left ( y , z \right ) \mathclose {}} \xrightarrow { { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} \right ) \mathclose {}} } \mathbf {hom} _{ B } { \mathopen {} \left ( x , z \right ) \mathclose {}} called horizontal composition;

for all 1-morphisms e \xrightarrow {f}x \xrightarrow {g}y \xrightarrow {h}z, an isomorphism { \mathopen {} \left ( f \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} g \right ) \mathclose {}} \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} h \xrightarrow { \alpha _{ f,g,h } } f \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} { \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} h \right ) \mathclose {}} natural in f,g,h called the associator;
for all 1-morphisms x \xrightarrow {f}y, an isomorphism 1 _{ x } \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} f \xrightarrow { \lambda _{ f } } f natural in f called the left unitor;
for all 1-morphisms x \xrightarrow {f}y, an isomorphism f \mathbin { \boldsymbol {;}} 1 _{ y } \xrightarrow { \rho _{ f } } f natural in f called the right unitor;

frontmatter.title: Strong morphisms of adjunctions and the Beck–Chevalley condition

mainmatter.p: The condition that a lax morphism of adjunctions be right strong is sometimes referred to as the Beck–Chevalley condition, e.g. by Uemura. Indeed, the right-hand mates in a lax morphism of adjunctions are occasionally referred to as Beck–Chevalley transformations.
